claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 3 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to start by learning about the child or children you're asking about.

**First question: How old is the child you're claiming for?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (subject to other conditions)
- Young people **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please tell me the age of the child (or if you have multiple children, let's start with one and we can assess each of them).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm actually asking about two of my children. I've got Alex who's 17, and Blake who's 3. Should I give you information about both of them, or would you like to go through them one at a time?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have two children - Alex (17) and Blake (3). I'll assess each child's eligibility, and since they're in different age categories, we'll need to go through different checks for each of them.

Let me start with **Blake (age 3)** since that assessment will be more straightforward, and then we'll move on to **Alex (age 17)**.

**For Blake (age 3):**

Since Blake is under 16, they generally qualify as a qualifying child. However, I need to check one important condition:

**Is Blake in full-time paid work (16+ hours per week)?**

(This would be very unusual for a 3-year-old, but I need to ask as part of the formal assessment!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - Blake's only 3! They're not working at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Blake is definitely not working. Let me continue with Blake's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Blake:**

**Is Blake receiving any benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

(Again, very unlikely for a 3-year-old, but I need to confirm!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't get any benefits in their own name. They're just 3, so nothing like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now one more check for Blake:

**Is Blake in any type of apprenticeship?**

(Again, not applicable for a 3-year-old, but I'm working through the formal checklist!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake's not in an apprenticeship - they're only 3!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we're moving into questions about **you as the claimant**. These questions will apply to both Blake and Alex, so I only need to ask them once.

**Residency requirement:**

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

Or if not, do any of these situations apply to you:
- You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- You have pre-settled status and are working, job seeking, studying, or have sufficient resources
- You're a Crown servant living abroad
- You're living in certain qualifying countries

In most cases, people simply live in the UK normally. Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! You meet the residency requirement.

**Next question about responsibility for the children:**

**Are you responsible for Blake and Alex?**

This means either:
- You live with them, OR
- You contribute to their upkeep (paying for food, clothes, housing, etc. - at least the equivalent of what Child Benefit would pay)

Do you live with Blake and Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Blake and Alex live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake or Alex?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- A grandparent or other family member

If someone else is claiming but you've agreed that you should claim instead, that's fine - we can proceed. But I need to know if there's currently another claimant.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check the children's living situation:

**For Blake and Alex, are either of them currently:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally at home with you?

What's the situation for both children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're both just living normally at home with me. Neither of them is in hospital, in care, or being fostered.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! One final check before we assess Blake's eligibility:

**Is the local council paying towards Blake or Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, are you receiving any payments from the local council specifically for their care or housing costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards their care or accommodation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news for **Blake**! Blake meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. ✓

Now let's assess **Alex (age 17)**. Since Alex is between 16-19 years old, there are some additional education and training requirements we need to check.

**For Alex (17 years old):**

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

This includes:
- Full-time non-advanced education (A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQ Level 3 or below, etc.)
- Approved training programs
- The education/training must be at least 12 hours per week of supervised study or course-related work experience

Is Alex in any kind of education or training program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. He left school and isn't doing A-levels or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Since Alex is 17 and not currently in approved education or training, let me check one more thing:

**Did Alex recently leave approved education or training?**

This is important because there's an "extension period" - you can continue to claim Child Benefit for a limited time after a young person leaves education, up until a specific date.

When did Alex leave school or education? And do you know the exact date he left?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He left school a while back - it's been several months now. He just finished and hasn't gone on to do anything else since then.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I need to get a more specific timeframe to properly assess Alex's eligibility. There's an extension period that allows Child Benefit to continue for a short time after leaving education.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Can you tell me approximately when Alex left school?** 

The extension period allows you to claim Child Benefit for up to **20 weeks** after a young person leaves approved education. So I need to know:

- What month did Alex leave school?
- Or roughly how many months ago was it?

This will help me determine if you're still within that 20-week extension period or if it has already expired.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been quite a bit longer than that - probably around 8 or 9 months ago now that he left school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Unfortunately, since Alex left school 8-9 months ago, that's well beyond the 20-week extension period (which is about 5 months).

Let me now provide you with the complete assessment for both of your children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results

---

### **Blake (age 3): ✅ ELIGIBLE**

**Good news!** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake. Blake meets all the requirements:
- Under 16 years old
- Not in full-time work
- Not receiving benefits in their own right
- Lives with you at home
- No competing claims

---

### **Alex (age 17): ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, you are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex at this time. Here's why:

- Alex is 17, which means he needs to be in **approved education or training** to qualify
- Alex left school 8-9 months ago and is not currently in education or training
- The extension period after leaving education is **20 weeks (about 5 months)**, which has now passed

**However, Alex could become eligible again if he:**
- Returns to approved full-time education (A-levels, T-levels, NVQs Level 3 or below, etc.)
- Starts an approved training program
- The course must be at least 12 hours per week of supervised study

---

## Next Steps

### For Blake:
**You should apply for Child Benefit!** You can:
- **Apply online** or by post using form CH2
- You'll need to provide:
  - Proof of your identity
  - Blake's birth certificate
  
**Important note about income:** If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (a tax charge). However, this doesn't affect your eligibility - you can still claim.

**Find out more and apply:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

### For Alex:
If Alex decides to return to education or start training, you can apply for Child Benefit at that point. Make sure the course is:
- Full-time (at least 12 hours per week)
- Non-advanced (not a university degree or Level 4+ qualification)

Do you have any questions about this assessment or the next steps?

reasonings: Blake is 3 years old, which is under 16. Blake is not in full-time paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. You live in the UK (Manchester), live with Blake, no one else is claiming for Blake, Blake lives normally at home with you, and the local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. Blake meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Alex is 17 years old, which falls in the 16-19 age bracket requiring approved education or training. Alex is not currently in approved education or training, and left school approximately 8-9 months ago. The extension period for Child Benefit after leaving education is 20 weeks (approximately 5 months). Since Alex left school 8-9 months ago, this exceeds the 20-week extension period. Therefore, Alex does not meet the qualifying child criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You meet all the claimant requirements (UK residency, responsibility for children, no competing claims). Blake qualifies as a child under 16. However, Alex at 17 does not qualify because he is not in approved education or training and has exceeded the 20-week extension period since leaving school.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.
